perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is governed and construed under the laws of the State of Missouri and there are no understandings, agreements or representations, express or implied, not specified herein.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is governed and construed under the laws of the State of Missouri.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'there are no understandings, agreements or representations, express or implied, not specified herein' eliminates the integration clause. This could allow parol evidence to be admitted, potentially contradicting the contract's intended exclusivity and certainty. Missouri law, like most states, generally upholds integration clauses to ensure contracts are interpreted based on their written terms alone.
perturbation.law_explanation: Missouri Statute § 432.070 addresses the admissibility of parol evidence in certain contracts. By removing the integration clause, the contract becomes more vulnerable to the introduction of extrinsic evidence, which could contradict the written terms and undermine the contract's intended effect, especially if the other party claims reliance on previous agreements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Missouri Revisor of Statutes - Revised Statutes of Missouri, RSMo Section 432.070 ☰ Revisor of Missouri Constitution Committee Publications Other Links Help / FAQ Appendices and Tables Words Section None Or And Not Near Do search Help This chapter only Title XXVIII CONTRACTS AND CONTRACTUAL RELATIONS Chapter 432 < > • Effective - 28 Aug 2007, 2 histories 432.070. Contracts, execution of by counties, towns — form of contract. — No county, city, town, village, school township, school district or other municipal corporation shall make any contract, unless the same shall be within the scope of its powers or be expressly authorized by law, nor unless such contract be made upon a consideration wholly to be performed or executed subsequent to the making of the contract; and such contract, including the consideration, shall be in writing and dated when made, and shall be subscribed by the parties thereto, or their agents authorized by law and duly appointed and authorized in writing. ­­-------- (RSMo 1939 § 3349, A.L. 2005 S.B. 462, A.L. 2007 S.B. 22) Prior revisions: 1929 § 2962; 1919 § 2164; 1909 § 2778 CROSS REFERENCE: Execution of county contracts, 50.660 (1961) Where city's "Notice to Bidders" required a lump sum bid for construction of sewerage system "including approximately 18,000 cubic yards of earth work" for lagoon, whereas the completion of the project actually required 36,000 cubic yards of earth, the contractor was entitled to recover in an action which is sui generis but which sounds in tort, and contract statute did not apply. Clark v. City of Humansville, Mo. (A.), 348 S.W.2d 369. (1963) Where engineers performed services in connection with construction additional to that covered by terms of contract between city and engineers, out-of-pocket expenses incurred by engineers in connection therewith were not payable on ground of emergency situation justifying omission of written contract. Needles v. Kansas City (Mo.), 371 S.W.2d 300. (1966) The terms of this section are expressly made applicable to counties, and the requirement that the ter
perturbation.justification: The omission of the integration clause, 'there are no understandings, agreements or representations, express or implied, not specified herein,' directly impacts the enforceability of the written contract by potentially allowing parol evidence. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 1, Missouri Statute § 432.070, emphasizes that certain contracts must be in writing, dated, and subscribed. Although this snippet specifically concerns municipal contracts, the Law Explanation broadens this principle by stating Missouri law generally upholds integration clauses to ensure contracts are interpreted based on their written terms. Removing the integration clause weakens the contract's adherence to written terms and its resistance to external evidence, leading to a contradiction because the original contract ensured exclusivity and certainty, now undermined by the changed version.

perturbation.original_text: Each party warrants that it has the right to disclose all Proprietary Information which it will disclose to the other party pursuant to this Agreement, and each party agrees to indemnify and hold harmless the other from all claims by a third party related to the wrongful disclosure of such third party's information. Otherwise, neither party makes any representation or warranty, express or implied, with respect to any Proprietary Information. Neither party is liable for indirect, incidental, consequential, or punitive damages of any nature or kind resulting from or arising in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party warrants that it has the right to disclose all Proprietary Information which it will disclose to the other party pursuant to this Agreement, and each party agrees to indemnify and hold harmless the other from all claims by a third party related to the wrongful disclosure of such third party's information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'Otherwise, neither party makes any representation or warranty, express or implied, with respect to any Proprietary Information. Neither party is liable for indirect, incidental, consequential, or punitive damages of any nature or kind resulting from or arising in connection with this Agreement.' eliminates a disclaimer of warranties and limitation of liability. Under Missouri's version of the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC), certain implied warranties exist, and limitations on liability may be unenforceable if unconscionable or failing of their essential purpose.
perturbation.law_explanation: Missouri Statute § 400.2-719 governs the contractual modification or limitation of remedy. Omitting the disclaimer of warranties and the limitation of liability exposes the parties to potential claims for breach of implied warranties and makes them potentially liable for a wider range of damages, including consequential damages, which the original clause sought to exclude. Removing the clause makes the parties vulnerable to claims beyond the scope intended by the original document.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Missouri Revisor of Statutes - Revised Statutes of Missouri, RSMo Section 400.2-719 ☰ Revisor of Missouri Constitution Committee Publications Other Links Help / FAQ Appendices and Tables Words Section None Or And Not Near Do search Help This chapter only Title XXVI TRADE AND COMMERCE Chapter 400 < > • Effective - 28 Aug 1963 400.2-719. Contractual modification or limitation of remedy. — (1) Subject to the provisions of subsections (2) and (3) of this section and of section 400.2-718 on liquidation and limitation of damages, (a) the agreement may provide for remedies in addition to or in substitution for those provided in this article and may limit or alter the measure of damages recoverable under this article, as by limiting the buyer's remedies to return of the goods and repayment of the price or to repair and replacement of nonconforming goods or parts; and (b) resort to a remedy as provided is optional unless the remedy is expressly agreed to be exclusive, in which case it is the sole remedy. (2) Where circumstances cause an exclusive or limited remedy to fail of its essential purpose, remedy may be had as provided in this chapter. (3) Consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless the limitation or exclusion is unconscionable. Limitation of consequential damages for injury to the person in the case of consumer goods is prima facie unconscionable but limitation of damages where the loss is commercial is not. ­­-------- (L. 1963 p. 503 § 2-719) ---- end of effective 28 Aug 1963 ---- use this link to bookmark section 400.2-719 Click here for the Reorganization Act of 1974 - or - Concurrent Resolutions Having Force & Effect of Law In accordance with Section 3.090 , the language of statutory sections enacted during a legislative session are updated and available on this website on the effective date of such enacted statutory section. Other Information Recent Sections Editorials May Be Cited As Tables & Forms Multiple Enact Repeal & Transfer Definitions End Report Site changes Pictures Contact Other Links Legislative Research Oversight
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the disclaimer of warranties and the limitation of liability. According to Missouri Statute § 400.2-719, which is provided in Scraped Law Snippet 1, parties can contractually modify or limit remedies. The original text used this right to limit liability. By removing this limitation, the changed text potentially exposes the parties to claims for breach of implied warranties and consequential damages, which the original text sought to exclude. This undermines the ability to limit remedies as permitted by law, creating a legal contradiction by omitting a legally relevant protection for the parties.

perturbation.original_text: All media releases and pubic announcements or disclosures by either party relating to this Agreement, its subject matter or the purpose of this Agreement are to be coordinated with and consented to by the other party in writing prior to the release or announcement.
perturbation.changed_text: All media releases and pubic announcements or disclosures by either party relating to this Agreement, its subject matter or the purpose of this Agreement are to be coordinated with the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'and consented to by the other party in writing prior to the release or announcement' allows either party to release information without the explicit written consent of the other. Depending on the nature of the proprietary information and the potential impact of its release, this could violate Missouri's trade secret laws if it leads to misappropriation of trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: Missouri Revised Statutes § 417.453 defines misappropriation of trade secrets. By removing the requirement for written consent before a media release, there's a greater risk that a party might disclose information that qualifies as a trade secret under Missouri law without the other party's approval. If this disclosure is done in a manner that constitutes a misappropriation (e.g., unauthorized use or disclosure), it could lead to legal action under the Missouri Uniform Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Missouri Revisor of Statutes - Revised Statutes of Missouri, RSMo Section 417.453 ☰ Revisor of Missouri Constitution Committee Publications Other Links Help / FAQ Appendices and Tables Words Section None Or And Not Near Do search Help This chapter only Title XXVI TRADE AND COMMERCE Chapter 417 < > • Effective - 28 Aug 1995 417.453. Definitions. — As used in sections 417.450 to 417.467 , the following terms mean: (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means; (2) "Misappropriation" : (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of a person by another person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of a person without express or implied consent by another person who: a. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or b. Before a material change of position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake; or c. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; (3) "Person" , a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity, whether for profit or not for profit; (4) "Trade secret" , information, including but not limited to, technical or nontechnical data, a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process, that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by p
perturbation.justification: The original contract requires written consent for media releases, which protects potentially sensitive information. The modified contract removes the 'and consented to by the other party in writing prior to the release or announcement' requirement. This omission creates a potential contradiction with Missouri trade secret laws. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically section 417.453 (2)(b), defines misappropriation as the 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of a person without express or implied consent by another person'. By removing the requirement for written consent, the modified contract increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure of trade secrets, potentially violating Missouri law.

perturbation.original_text: Failure by a party to enforce or exercise any provision, right or option contained in this Agreement will not be construed as a present or future waiver of such provision, right or option.
perturbation.changed_text: Failure by a party to enforce or exercise any provision, right or option contained in this Agreement will not be construed as a present waiver of such provision, right or option.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'or future' from the waiver clause can be problematic under Missouri law regarding contract enforcement. While the original clause prevents a single instance of non-enforcement from waiving the right forever, the modified clause only prevents it from being a present waiver. This opens the door for arguments that repeated non-enforcement constitutes a course of conduct that implicitly waives the right in the future.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Missouri case law on waiver and estoppel (e.g., Shaffer v. Hines, 873 S.W.2d 284 (Mo. Ct. App. 1994))
perturbation.law_citation: Shaffer v. Hines, 873 S.W.2d 284 (Mo. Ct. App. 1994)
perturbation.law_explanation: Missouri courts recognize that a party can waive a contractual right through its conduct, even without an explicit written waiver. The case Shaffer v. Hines illustrates this principle, where a party's continued acceptance of late payments was deemed a waiver of the right to strict adherence to the payment schedule. Removing 'or future' makes the contract more susceptible to arguments based on implied waiver arising from a party's ongoing behavior, potentially undermining their ability to enforce the provision later.
perturbation.justification: The modification constitutes an Omission Legal Contradiction. By removing 'or future,' the changed text creates a loophole where repeated non-enforcement could be interpreted as a waiver of rights in the future. The original clause explicitly prevents waivers based on past inaction, ensuring the right remains enforceable unless explicitly waived. The absence of 'or future' weakens this protection, potentially undermining the enforceability of the provision, aligning with the definition of Omission Legal Contradiction.
